quote: that there was, a way to get this the Tesla roof tiles. Is that is that the one that you're calling about?
stewart_read: Rocky opens with a specific Tesla roof tile expectation — the narrowest version of the expectation gap the schema flags. Brandon's response is a flat denial ('No. This would be the you have a form for solar panels') with no reframe. The schema's correct handling is: acknowledge the referral, clarify Ion is Tesla's installer partner, note that Ion carries its own panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery, and that the closer walks through options. Brandon skips all of this and pivots to the form. Strategic read: Brandon may have been trying to move past the confusion quickly to avoid scope creep. Reactive read: the flat denial without reframe leaves Rocky's expectation gap unresolved — he never learns what Ion actually offers relative to what he was hoping for.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon's quick pivot was deliberate scope discipline — don't over-explain the Tesla relationship at intro, just move to qualify.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the schema's tesla_relationship_clarification reframe. The move isn't a long explanation — it's one sentence: 'Tesla referred your inquiry to us as their installer partner in Illinois; we install our own panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery, and the closer walks through the full package.' That sentence closes the expectation gap without scope creep. The flat denial risks Rocky feeling misdirected before the call has started.

quote: In the mail. Yeah. So it's, but I have a copy of it. It's roughly, you know, my average over the last year is somewhere around 380 a month.
stewart_read: Rocky volunteers his average monthly bill unprompted — $380/month — and Brandon responds with 'Okay. Great.' and immediately pivots to explaining what the bill photo will be used for. This is the schema's universal failure pattern verbatim: 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The $380 number is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch and Brandon treats it as a data point to be filed, not a pain point to be amplified. The flip was available: Rocky's $380/month is the expense solar replaces, not a number to acknowledge and move past.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been holding the emotional anchor for the appointment itself, letting the closer deploy it with the design in hand.
coaching_implication: This is the textbook bill-anchor-without-flip moment. In the 1-on-1, play the tape from 03:40 to 04:09 and ask Brandon: 'What did Rocky just tell you, and what did you do with it?' The suggested rephrase is intent-based, not scripted: acknowledge the number, then redirect it — something like 'Three-eighty a month — that's the number we're trying to eliminate, not just reduce. Let's see what we can do.' No fabricated savings figures needed. The pain the number represents is the leverage.

quote: Yeah. I mean, I'll tell you, like, when the sun run, I mean, they were able to only save me they weren't able to save me much. Like, it was yeah. It'll reduce my bill by a little bit, but that's it. So I'm curious to see if if you can offset it.
stewart_read: Rocky's Sunrun disappointment is not an objection — it's a buying signal. He's explicitly telling Brandon that the prior experience left him unsatisfied and he's open to a better outcome. This is the highest-leverage moment in the call. Brandon's response ('Yeah. Typically, we want to offset your entire bill') is factually correct but emotionally inert. The flip was available: Rocky's frustration with Sunrun's partial offset is the exact emotional fuel the schema's objection_inversion is designed to use. The move isn't to explain Ion's approach — it's to validate Rocky's frustration and redirect it toward curiosity about what Ion's design will show.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon's factual answer was deliberate scope discipline — don't over-promise, let the closer show the numbers.
coaching_implication: The factual answer was right; the emotional flatness was the miss. In the 1-on-1, ask Brandon: 'Rocky just told you Sunrun disappointed him and he's hoping you can do better — what's the move there?' The suggested rephrase is curiosity-based: 'That's exactly why we're having this conversation. Sunrun's cap at 20k on a 26k-usage home is a design question — let's see what our specialist puts together for your roof specifically.' No numbers invented. The curiosity bridge is the move, not the explanation.

quote: Yeah. Typically, we want to offset your entire bill. Because if we can't, then it doesn't make sense for you to go solar.
stewart_read: Brandon's answer is scope-appropriate — he correctly defers the actual design and numbers to the specialist rather than quoting savings ranges. The thin answer is the right call. The coaching gap is what's missing after the answer: there's no curiosity bridge to the appointment. Brandon answers Rocky's question and then pauses, waiting for Rocky to move the conversation forward. The setter's job at this moment is to convert Rocky's curiosity into appointment urgency — 'that's exactly what our specialist will show you' — not to answer and wait.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon was intentionally keeping the answer brief to avoid scope creep, planning to pivot immediately to the booking ask.
coaching_implication: The thin answer was right; the missing bridge was the gap. Show Brandon the schema's universal escape hatch: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer.' The move is to answer briefly and immediately redirect to the appointment as the venue where Rocky's question gets fully resolved. Brandon did the first half and skipped the second.

quote: We'll have a design ready before we meet with you, and we'll explore a few options. We guarantee savings, so we'd love to show you what we're offering to see if it'll fit your needs.
stewart_read: Two distinct problems in one sentence. First, 'we guarantee savings' is an ungrounded promise the setter cannot make — the design hasn't been built, the bill hasn't been received, and the closer hasn't run the numbers. If the closer can't deliver on this promise, the appointment becomes a trust-damage event. Second, 'we'll have a design ready before we meet with you' is preparation language, not advance delivery — this does not trigger the schema's rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting hard rule, which applies only to customer requests for advance delivery. The actual coaching gap here is promise discipline, not proposal protocol.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have intended 'guarantee savings' as a confidence signal rather than a literal contractual promise.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, isolate 'we guarantee savings' and ask Brandon: 'What happens if the closer runs the numbers and the savings are marginal?' The suggested rephrase replaces the promise with a curiosity bridge: 'We'll have a design built around your specific usage — the specialist will show you exactly what the numbers look like for your home.' Same confidence, no ungrounded promise. The schema's numeric_discipline principle applies: the pain the number represents is the leverage, not the promise of a number.

quote: Sounds good. Okay. So I'll, I'll send you this thing, and I'll look forward to seeing you at 01:00 on or seeing the technician at 01:00 on, Thursday the twenty third.
stewart_read: The booking is confirmed but the button-up protocol is incomplete on multiple dimensions. No one-hour block stated. No phone-appointment specification. No computer/no-driving anchor. No 'is that fair' verbal handshake. Rocky's self-correction ('seeing you' to 'seeing the technician') suggests mild format ambiguity but not a hard misunderstanding — he knows a specialist is coming. More critically: title/co-owners was never asked during verify, so there's no information about whether a spouse or co-decision-maker exists. If someone else is on the title, this appointment is structurally vulnerable.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have planned to send the button-up details via text, covering the missing anchors in the follow-up message.
coaching_implication: Walk Brandon through the button-up checklist against this call: one-hour block, phone appointment, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake. Four of five elements are missing. More importantly, ask Brandon: 'Did you ask Rocky if anyone else is on the title?' The schema's verify.title_co_owners item is the critical missing qualifier — if there's a spouse, this appointment needs to include them or it's structurally at risk.

quote: Yeah. So I I can tell you right now that, you know, I talked to at least one that so we use up about 26,000 kilowatt hours for a year. And the first estimate I got was that they can only generate 20,000 kilowatt hours, even though we have no, you know, obstructions on the roof.
stewart_read: Rocky volunteers precise annual kWh usage, a prior Sunrun quote, the specific shortfall (6,000 kWh unmet), and the absence of roof obstructions — all unprompted, in the first two minutes. This is a highly qualified buyer who has done homework and is actively benchmarking. Brandon's response is to ask who Rocky met with and why Sunrun capped at 20k — useful qualification, but the enthusiasm signal (a buyer who already knows his numbers and is frustrated with a competitor's partial solution) is never amplified. The schema's verify.interest_in_solar depth-grading principle applies: this is a 60-second emotional anchor opportunity that Brandon treats as a data-collection moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon's investigative questions about Sunrun were deliberate — surface the competitive context before pitching.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon that Rocky's opening data dump is a buying signal, not just qualification data. The move after 'we use 26,000 kWh and Sunrun could only do 20,000' is to acknowledge the frustration before asking the follow-up question: 'That gap — 6,000 kWh you're still paying for — that's exactly what we'd want to close. Let me ask you a couple of things about your setup.' Brandon asked the right follow-up questions but skipped the emotional acknowledgment that would have built rapport and set up the appointment ask.
